were a [Negro league baseball](/wiki/Negro_league_baseball "Negro league baseball") team that played from 1920 until 1960, including 18 seasons recognized as Major League by [Major League Baseball](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball "Major League Baseball").[[1]](#cite_note-1) They shared their home field of [Rickwood Field](/wiki/Rickwood_Field "Rickwood Field") in [Birmingham, Alabama](/wiki/Birmingham,_Alabama "Birmingham, Alabama"), with the white [Birmingham Barons](/wiki/Birmingham_Barons "Birmingham Barons"), usually drawing larger crowds and equal press.

["MLB officially designates the Negro Leagues as 'Major League'"](https://www.mlb.com/press-release/press-release-mlb-officially-designates-the-negro-leagues-as-major-league). *MLB.com*. Retrieved July 29, 2024.

The **Birmingham Black Barons** were a [Negro league baseball](/wiki/Negro_league_baseball "Negro league baseball") team that played from 1920 until 1960, including 18 seasons recognized as Major League by [Major League Baseball](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball "Major League Baseball").[[1]](#cite_note-1) They shared their home field of [Rickwood Field](/wiki/Rickwood_Field "Rickwood Field") in [Birmingham, Alabama](/wiki/Birmingham,_Alabama "Birmingham, Alabama"), with the white [Birmingham Barons](/wiki/Birmingham_Barons "Birmingham Barons"), usually drawing larger crowds and equal press.

## Founding

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Birmingham_Black_Barons&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Founding")]

Drawing largely from a successful [American Cast Iron Pipe Company](/wiki/American_Cast_Iron_Pipe_Company "American Cast Iron Pipe Company") [Industrial League](/w/index.php?title=Industrial_League&action=edit&redlink=1 "Industrial League (page does not exist)") team, the Black Barons were organized in 1920 for the inaugural season of [Rube Foster](/wiki/Rube_Foster "Rube Foster")'s [Negro Southern League](/wiki/Negro_Southern_League_(1920%E2%80%9336) "Negro Southern League (1920–36)"), which operated mainly as a minor league. They played in that league for three years before making the leap to the larger [Negro National League](/wiki/Negro_National_League_(1920-1931) "Negro National League (1920-1931)"), which operated as a major league. They were unable to keep their position due to irregularities with the team finances and returned to the Southern League for three more years. Their return to the National League in 1927 was marked by the emergence of star pitcher [Satchel Paige](/wiki/Satchel_Paige "Satchel Paige"), who led the Black Barons to the second half pennant. They lost the Negro National League title to the [Chicago American Giants](/wiki/Chicago_American_Giants "Chicago American Giants") in four straight games.

## Later years

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Birmingham_Black_Barons&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Later years")]

For the next decade or so they alternated leagues before being bought by [Memphis, Tennessee](/wiki/Memphis,_Tennessee "Memphis, Tennessee") funeral home director [Tom Hayes](/wiki/Tom_Hayes_(baseball) "Tom Hayes (baseball)") in December 1939.[[2]](#cite_note-Black_Barons_To_Run_Next_Season_Under_New_Setup-2) The club joined the [Negro American League](/wiki/Negro_American_League "Negro American League") in 1940. Early in the decade the team was sold again to [Abraham Saperstein](/wiki/Abraham_Saperstein "Abraham Saperstein") who also owned the [Harlem Globetrotters](/wiki/Harlem_Globetrotters "Harlem Globetrotters") basketball team. In 1943 and 1944 they won back-to-back pennants. Starting in 1945, they became full members of the Negro American League and continued their success, winning a third pennant in 1948 with the help of teenage outfielder [Willie Mays](/wiki/Willie_Mays "Willie Mays"). They ended up losing three [Negro World Series](/wiki/Negro_World_Series "Negro World Series") to the [Homestead Grays](/wiki/Homestead_Grays "Homestead Grays") that decade, forging a notable rivalry. As the National and American Leagues started signing talented African American players, the Black Barons tried to form a [new Negro Southern League](/wiki/Negro_Southern_League_(1945%E2%80%9351) "Negro Southern League (1945–51)") with three other Southern teams.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The franchise was owned by William Bridgeforth from 1952 to 1955, and by Sid Lynor and Floyd Meshac in 1955. Dr. Anderson Ross purchased the franchise in 1956 and renamed the team the Birmingham Giants.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

The Black Barons played their last game in 1960.

## Throwback games

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Birmingham_Black_Barons&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Throwback games")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7f/Coming_in_from_Warmup.jpg/250px-Coming_in_from_Warmup.jpg)](/wiki/File:Coming_in_from_Warmup.jpg)

Black Barons 2006

The 1999 [Rickwood Classic](/wiki/Rickwood_Field#Current_status "Rickwood Field") honored the Black Barons, with the [Birmingham Barons](/wiki/Birmingham_Barons "Birmingham Barons") and [Huntsville Stars](/wiki/Huntsville_Stars "Huntsville Stars") wearing throwback uniforms. Some 35 former Negro leagues players, including former Black Baron [Charley Pride](/wiki/Charley_Pride "Charley Pride"), attended.

On February 26, 2006, [ESPN Classic](/wiki/ESPN_Classic "ESPN Classic") broadcast a throwback game from [Rickwood Field](/wiki/Rickwood_Field "Rickwood Field") featuring amateur players in the uniforms of the Birmingham Black Barons and fictitious "Bristol Barnstormers". The style of play, the equipment and the umpires all reflected the 1940s game. Willie Mays and Charley Pride were both in attendance. The Black Barons rallied to break an eighth inning tie and win the game, 9–8.

On June 18, 2024, as part of Major League Baseball's Tribute to the Negro Leagues event held at Rickwood Field, the Birmingham Barons and [Montgomery Biscuits](/wiki/Montgomery_Biscuits "Montgomery Biscuits") played as their Negro League predecessors of the Birmingham Black Barons and Montgomery Gray Sox; the game was broadcast live on the [MLB Network](/wiki/MLB_Network "MLB Network"). Of particular note is that the game was paused in the bottom of the 7th inning for the announcement that baseball great Willie Mays had died.[[4]](#cite_note-4) Montgomery won the game 6-5.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

## Notable players

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Birmingham_Black_Barons&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Notable players")]

In addition to [Satchel Paige](/wiki/Satchel_Paige "Satchel Paige"), [Willie Mays](/wiki/Willie_Mays "Willie Mays") also played as center fielder during both the 1948 and 1949 seasons. [Mule Suttles](/wiki/Mule_Suttles "Mule Suttles") was a member of the Black Barons in 1924 and 1925 seasons. Suttles was elected to the [Baseball Hall of Fame](/wiki/Baseball_Hall_of_Fame "Baseball Hall of Fame") in 2006. Other players, like [Artie Wilson](/wiki/Artie_Wilson "Artie Wilson"), [Bill Greason](/wiki/Bill_Greason "Bill Greason"), and [Jay Heard](/wiki/Jay_Heard "Jay Heard") also saw limited time (under 20 games each) in the Major Leagues.

### Hall of Fame players

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Birmingham_Black_Barons&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Hall of Fame players")]

The following Black Barons players have been inducted into the [National Baseball Hall of Fame](/wiki/National_Baseball_Hall_of_Fame "National Baseball Hall of Fame") in [Cooperstown, New York](/wiki/Cooperstown,_New_York "Cooperstown, New York").

| Birmingham Black Barons Hall of Famers | | | |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Inductee | Position | Tenure | Inducted |
| [Willie Mays](/wiki/Willie_Mays "Willie Mays") | [CF](/wiki/Center_fielder "Center fielder") | 1948–1949 | 1979 |
| [Satchel Paige](/wiki/Satchel_Paige "Satchel Paige") | [P](/wiki/Pitcher "Pitcher") | 1927–1930 | 1971 |
| [Mule Suttles](/wiki/Mule_Suttles "Mule Suttles") | [1B](/wiki/First_baseman "First baseman") | 1924–1925 | 2006 |
| [Willie Wells](/wiki/Willie_Wells "Willie Wells") | [SS](/wiki/Shortstop "Shortstop") | 1941 | 1997 |

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Birmingham_Black_Barons&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Other star players")]

* [Dan Bankhead](/wiki/Dan_Bankhead "Dan Bankhead") (1940–1942, 1944)
* [Sam Bankhead](/wiki/Sam_Bankhead "Sam Bankhead") (1931–1932)
* [Lyman Bostock Sr.](/wiki/Lyman_Bostock_Sr. "Lyman Bostock Sr.") (1940–1942, 1946)
* [Piper Davis](/wiki/Piper_Davis "Piper Davis") (1942–1950)
* [Bill Greason](/wiki/Bill_Greason "Bill Greason") (1948–1949)
* [Jay Heard](/wiki/Jay_Heard "Jay Heard") (1946–1948)
* [Jimmy Newberry](/wiki/Jimmy_Newberry "Jimmy Newberry") (1944–1950)
* [Charley Pride](/wiki/Charley_Pride "Charley Pride") (1953)
* [Artie Wilson](/wiki/Artie_Wilson "Artie Wilson") (1944–1948)
